retrieved_docs.title: Atrioventricular Block -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: An AV block could be congenital or acquired. Congenital AV block can be associated with congenital heart diseases, including AV canal defects, transposition of great arteries, heterotaxy syndrome, left atrial isomerism, and tetralogy of Fallot. [8] [9] Congenital AV block without structural heart disease, is relatively more common than those associated with congenital heart disease. Immune-mediated conduction system dysfunction is the main reason for congenital AV block in patients without congenital heart disease. [10] Systemic lupus erythematosus is one of the common autoimmune diseases associated with congenital AV block. [11] Maternal viral infection is another cause of congenital AV block in offspring. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Lupus Erythematosus -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Other causes of fetal bradycardia or heart block include congenital heart block defects such as L-transposition of the great arteries, endocardial cushion defects, Holt-Oram syndrome, and idiopathic familial congenital heart block. Differentiating these structural or genetic causes from autoimmune-mediated block is essential, as management strategies and prognoses differ significantly. Neonatal sepsis may present with rash and signs of heart failure, but the timing and characteristics of the rash typically differ. For example, TORCH infections (toxoplasmosis, other [including syphilis], rubella, cytomegalovirus, and herpes simplex virus) can cause a "blueberry muffin" rash associated with thrombocytopenia. A thorough maternal history and targeted diagnostic testing are essential to distinguish these conditions from neonatal lupus. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Lupus Erythematosus -- History and Physical -- Cardiac Manifestations
retrieved_docs.content: Newborns with NLE typically have a structurally normal heart. Cardiac involvement, present in approximately 25% of cases, is the most distinctive manifestation and most often presents as congenital heart block, which may be first-, second, or, most commonly, third-degree AV block. Congenital heart block is usually detected in utero between 18 and 24 weeks' gestation, presenting as fetal bradycardia (ventricular rates 40–80 bpm). Other cardiac manifestations include sinus bradycardia, QT-interval prolongation, cardiomyopathy, congestive heart failure, myocarditis, and structural or valvular defects such as ventricular septal defects, ostium secundum type atrial septal defects, patent foramen ovale, patent ductus arteriosus, pulmonary stenosis, pulmonary valve dysplasia, and fusion of the chordae tendineae of the tricuspid valve. [7] [27] [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Lupus Erythematosus -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxychloroquine (400 mg daily), initiated between 6 and 10 weeks' gestation, has been associated with a reduced risk of neonatal cardiac lupus, especially in women with a prior affected child. Beta-receptor agonists may improve fetal heart rate and stroke volume, but do not reduce the risk of AV block development. A ventricular rate of less than 55 per minute, hydrops fetalis, cardiomegaly, atrioventricular valve regurgitation, or low aortic flow velocity indicates poor fetal prognosis and outcome. Temporary pacing may be used while awaiting definitive therapy. Infants with a first-degree or stable second-degree block may be observed, as conduction may improve with the clearance of maternal antibodies. Long-term cardiology follow-up is essential. Despite intervention, up to 15% may develop late-onset cardiomyopathy.

retrieved_docs.title: Atrioventricular Block -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital AV block mediated by the immune system results from the transfer of maternal antibodies (anti-Ro and anti-La) across the placenta into the fetal circulation. These antibodies bind to fetal myocytes and impair cytoplasmic calcium metabolism leading to apoptotic cell death in the conduction system tissues. [10] The result of this immune-mediated process is the fibrosis of the cardiac conduction system and surrounding myocardium. [23] The presence of congenital AV block in individuals with congenital structural heart diseases can be attributed to abnormal development of the central fibrous body and abnormal development or disruption of the AV conduction tissue. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Lupus Erythematosus -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Most common and serious complication is congenital heart block (especially third-degree AV block)

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Lupus Erythematosus -- Evaluation -- Electrocardiogram
retrieved_docs.content: All infants with suspected NLE should undergo an electrocardiogram (ECG) to evaluate for AV block (prolonged PR interval), bundle branch block, or other rhythm disturbances. A normal ECG at birth is generally reassuring; however, if there was a known in utero first- or second-degree block that resolved, serial ECGs are recommended during the neonatal period, as late progression can occur. The risk of AV block is higher in infants born to mothers with a history of NLE-associated cardiac manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Lupus Erythematosus -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital heart block is permanent and often requires pacemaker placement

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Lupus Erythematosus -- Evaluation -- Echocardiography
retrieved_docs.content: Echocardiography is essential for both fetal screening and postnatal evaluation. In at-risk pregnancies (maternal anti-Ro/SSA or anti-La/SSA positivity), serial fetal echocardiograms are recommended beginning at age 16 weeks' gestation. Fewer than 20% of cases are detected after 26 weeks; thus, screening frequency may be reduced beyond that point if no abnormalities are identified. Fetal echocardiography can assess conduction delays via Doppler measurement of the mechanical PR interval and detect early signs of heart block or myocarditis, such as fetal bradycardia, atrial echogenic nodules (calcifications), or valvular regurgitation.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Lupus Erythematosus -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital heart block is permanent, but can be managed with pacing. Families should be counseled on skin care, emphasizing strict sun protection during early infancy. Clear instructions should be provided for recognizing signs of heart failure, such as poor feeding or lethargy, to enable prompt intervention. Ongoing follow-up with pediatric cardiology and other specialists is critical. Mothers should be informed of the recurrence risk in future pregnancies and the role of hydroxychloroquine in significantly reducing the risk. The long-term outlook for affected infants is generally favorable with multidisciplinary care and proper surveillance.

retrieved_docs.title: Second-Degree Atrioventricular Block  -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Differential diagnosis includes congenital heart block, sinoatrial exit block, non-conducted premature atrial complexes, first-degree atrioventricular block, myocardial infarction, medication toxicity, and third-degree atrioventricular block.

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia for Labor, Delivery, and Cesarean Section in High-Risk Heart Disease -- Introduction -- Risk Stratification
retrieved_docs.content: Ideally, developing an anesthetic plan for a high-risk cardiac patient should begin preconception or early in pregnancy so the patient can better comprehend the risks involved with pregnancy and the anesthetic plan that may be best for the expectant mother. Risk stratification and discussion amongst an interprofessional team of physicians are especially important for patients with congenital heart disease. [3] [4] A wide array of risk stratification tools are available to help the physician direct the intensity of an anesthetic plan and the patient toward hospitals of varying resources. Examples of these risk stratification tools include Cardiac Disease in Pregnancy (CARPEG) I & II, Zwangerschap bij Aangeboren Hartafwijking (ZAHARA), and World Health Organization (WHO) risk stratification calculators. CARPREG I & II and ZAHARA determine the likelihood of a significant cardiac event based on individual maternal risk factors. In contrast, the WHO risk stratification system divides individuals into 4 risk categories. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia for Labor, Delivery, and Cesarean Section in High-Risk Heart Disease -- Clinical Significance -- Unstable Arrhythmia History
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy, labor, as well as delivery predispose patients to tachyarrhythmias. Cardiac dysrhythmias discovered during pregnancy are usually benign. Cardioversion can be performed in pregnancy without harming the fetus; fetal distress is a good indicator of the need for cardioversion. The ventricular rate can be safely controlled with verapamil and beta-blocking agents. For hemodynamically stable supraventricular tachycardia, intravenous adenosine can be employed (3 to 12 mg). Patients should be evaluated for a pacemaker and an automatic implantable cardioverter-defibrillator. The pacemaker and automatic implantable cardioverter-defibrillator should be kept functioning during labor for a vaginal delivery, but a contingency plan should be in place in case of conversion to cesarean section; electrocautery interference can disrupt the function of the device. A magnet can usually convert devices to asynchronous mode (VOO). [7] Epidural anesthesia is associated with a decreased risk of mothers in the peripartum period developing dysrhythmia, making it an ideal modality for pain control. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Atrioventricular Block -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The precise prevalence of AV blocks remains unknown due to the absence of extensive population-based studies focusing specifically on their occurrence. The estimated occurrence of autoantibody-mediated congenital AV block is approximately 1 in 15,000 births. However, it is important to note that the actual incidence may be underestimated due to cases resulting in intrauterine deaths. [21] An AV block due to degeneration of cardiac conduction tissue is much more common in individuals older than 65. In contrast, the prevalence of third-degree AV block is rare in young individuals. [13] However, one study suggested that first-degree AV block is more prevalent in African-American patients than Caucasian patients in all age groups except in the eighth decade of life. [22] However, at this time, there is no well-characterized large study about the correlation between different types of AV block with age, race, or gender.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Lupus Erythematosus -- History and Physical -- Cardiac Manifestations
retrieved_docs.content: Affected neonates may have persistent bradycardia or present with signs of congestive heart failure (poor feeding, diaphoresis, pallor, edema, lethargy, tachypnea, hepatomegaly, prominent jugular veins, and crackles on auscultation of the lungs). Cardiac auscultation may reveal a slow, regular heart rate, variable S1 intensity, or fixed split S2. Five percent to 10% of infants present with a severe AV block that precipitates myocardial dysfunction due to endocardial fibroelastosis and myocardial fibrosis, associated with right ventricular pacing and subsequent ventricular asynchrony and dysfunction. [1] [27] [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Holt-Oram Syndrome -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Individuals with Holt-Oram syndrome are also at an increased risk for cardiac conduction abnormalities. These may manifest at birth as sinus bradycardia or first-degree atrioventricular block and can progress to complete heart block without warning. Arrhythmias, such as atrial fibrillation, are also seen. Life expectancy for Holt-Oram syndrome varies among affected individuals and predominantly depends on the severity of the congenital heart defect.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2040
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac complications of anesthesia or other sedation in labor and delivery, delivered, with mention of postpartum complication represent a more specific subset within the broader category of مضاعفات الحمل (pregnancy complications). Likewise, اضطراب جنيني (fetal disorder) is also classified under مضاعفات الحمل, indicating that fetal disorders are considered a type of pregnancy complication. Together, these relationships show that both anesthesia‑related cardiac events during labor and fetal disorders are encompassed by the overarching concept of pregnancy complications, underscoring the need for careful obstetric monitoring and management of these distinct yet related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2846
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of an allowed qualifier is applied to the prevention and control of mitral valve stenosis, threatened abortion, and fetal distress syndrome. Preventive and control strategies for mitral valve stenosis aim to limit valve narrowing and reduce cardiac complications. In cases of threatened abortion, early identification and appropriate interventions are essential to prevent progression to miscarriage and to protect maternal and fetal health. For fetal distress syndrome, vigilant monitoring and timely therapeutic measures are crucial to manage fetal compromise and improve outcomes for both mother and child. Integrating prevention and control measures for these conditions supports better patient care and reduces the risk of adverse events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: and cardiovascular complications are a subset of the overall perinatal condition spectrum. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 189
retrieved_docs.content: each condition as a potential adverse event, enabling systematic risk assessment, targeted monitoring, operative decision‑making, and coordinated maternal‑fetal and newborn care aimed at reducing morbidity and improving outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 109
retrieved_docs.content: disorders, and obstetric interventions together form a comprehensive framework for risk assessment, care planning, and management of maternal‑fetal outcomes during pregnancy, delivery, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 181
retrieved_docs.content: terminology (Neonatal Research Network Terminology), illustrating the interconnected nature of electrical propagation abnormalities, rapid heart‑rate disorders, their anatomical and autonomic substrates, associated structural heart disease, and relevant pharmacologic and procedural interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2587
retrieved_docs.content: Disease of circulatory system complicating pregnancy childbirth and puerperium is recognized as a specific type of اضطراب الحمل (pregnancy disorder), indicating that اضطراب الحمل represents a circulatory disease that can arise during pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium. At the same time, اضطراب الحمل functions as a broader parent category for Female Reproductive System Disorder, meaning that disorders of the female reproductive system are classified under the umbrella of pregnancy disorder. This dual positioning of اضطراب الحمل underscores the close link between cardiovascular complications that occur in the context of pregnancy and the wider spectrum of reproductive‑system disorders, highlighting the need for integrated evaluation and management of pregnant patients who present with both circulatory and reproductive health concerns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 129
retrieved_docs.content: outcome category ولادة جنين ميت (delivery of a dead fetus), indicating that the combined risks of surgical termination, severe hemorrhage, placental abruption, and placenta previa contribute to the possibility of stillbirth. Together, these entities and relationships illustrate how specific amniotic cavity problems, early membrane rupture, cesarean delivery, missed abortion, anesthesia‑related cardiac and central nervous system complications, fetal disorders, and termination‑related hemorrhagic conditions are interlinked with pregnancy complications and their late effects, ultimately influencing the risk of delivering a non‑viable infant and underscoring the importance of careful obstetric monitoring and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2099
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital vascular dilatation (تمدد الأوعية الدموية الفطري) is classified as a type of valvular heart disease (مرض الصمام القلبي). This condition involves abnormal dilation of blood vessels that is present from birth and is considered within the spectrum of diseases affecting heart valves. Recognizing congenital vascular dilatation as a valvular heart disease aids clinicians in diagnosing and monitoring potential valve dysfunction, altered hemodynamics, and related complications such as heart failure or arrhythmias, thereby guiding appropriate management and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2081
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac complications that arise from anesthesia or other sedation administered during labor and delivery, especially when accompanied by a postpartum complication, are categorized as “Other and unspecified complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother.” This broader category is further classified under the concept of “التأثير المتأخر لمضاعفات الحمل والولادة والنفاس,” which refers to the delayed effects of pregnancy, childbirth, and postpartum complications. Consequently, such cardiac events are understood as part of the spectrum of maternal complications that can influence postpartum care and require specific attention in the management of the mother after delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 944
retrieved_docs.content: be used to treat تسرع القلب الانتيابي, but it is contraindicated in إِحْصار القلب غير محدد, indicating that while adenosine can terminate certain tachycardias, it should be avoided in patients with unspecified heart block due to the risk of exacerbating conduction failure. Together, these relationships delineate a network in which various rhythm disorders, their anatomical substrates, therapeutic options, and contraindications are interlinked, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing cardiac conduction and rhythm abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are clinically associated with إنهاء الحمل – اختياري (optional termination of pregnancy) and also have a relationship with اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder). This indicates that uterine disorders are considered among the other complications of pregnancy and that optional termination of pregnancy can be clinically linked to these pregnancy‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2873
retrieved_docs.content: The condition referred to as اضطراب جنيني (fetal disorder) is classified as a type of مضاعفات الحمل (pregnancy complication). Recognizing that fetal disorders are encompassed within the broader category of pregnancy complications helps clinicians identify, monitor, and manage these issues during prenatal care, ensuring appropriate assessment and intervention for both maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 169
retrieved_docs.content: and abnormal forces of labor, illustrating how fetal factors can influence maternal care. Overall, the framework links broad diagnostic categories to precise maternal and fetal conditions, supporting accurate coding, clinical awareness, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 850
retrieved_docs.content: care during pregnancy, labor, and delivery. This integrated framework supports comprehensive obstetric assessment by recognizing how fetal presentation anomalies and maternal surgical history jointly inform the need for specialized care.
